---
title: "10 · The Pythagorean Theorem"
subtitle: "The deepest fact about right triangles — proved by rearranging squares"
lesson-id: "t2.pythagorean-theorem"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t1.shapes-angles", "t1.area-perimeter-volume", "t2.square-roots-irrationals"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
   and [09 · Square Roots](../09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)?
2. Do you know what a *right triangle* is — a triangle with one 90°
   corner?
3. Comfortable with $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ as algebra?
:::

## Touch

Drag the two shorter sides $a$ and $b$ of a right triangle. The
hypotenuse $c$ updates — and the squares built on each side show the
theorem in action.

```{ojs}
viewof aSide = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "a (vertical leg):"})
viewof bSide = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "b (horizontal leg):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const c = Math.sqrt(aSide**2 + bSide**2);
  const W = 720, H = 480, scale = 22;
  const ox = 200, oy = 250;  // top-right corner of the right angle
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px; background:#fafafa;");

  // Triangle vertices
  const A = {x: ox, y: oy - aSide * scale};       // top of vertical leg
  const B = {x: ox, y: oy};                        // right angle
  const C = {x: ox + bSide * scale, y: oy};        // right end of horizontal leg

  // Square on a (left)
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", ox - aSide * scale).attr("y", oy - aSide * scale)
    .attr("width", aSide * scale).attr("height", aSide * scale)
    .attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("opacity", 0.6).attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", ox - aSide * scale / 2).attr("y", oy - aSide * scale / 2 + 6)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c")
    .text(`a² = ${aSide**2}`);

  // Square on b (bottom)
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", ox).attr("y", oy)
    .attr("width", bSide * scale).attr("height", bSide * scale)
    .attr("fill", "#a5d6a7").attr("opacity", 0.6).attr("stroke", "#2e7d32");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", ox + bSide * scale / 2).attr("y", oy + bSide * scale / 2 + 6)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#2e7d32")
    .text(`b² = ${bSide**2}`);

  // Square on hypotenuse — rotated rectangle
  // Hypotenuse goes from A (top of vertical) to C (end of horizontal)
  // The square sits *outside* the triangle, on the hypotenuse
  // Direction along hypotenuse from A to C
  const dx = C.x - A.x, dy = C.y - A.y;
  // Perpendicular (outward from triangle) — rotate 90° clockwise from AC direction
  const px = -dy, py = dx;  // rotate (dx, dy) by 90° → (-dy, dx) outward
  const len = Math.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy);
  const nx = px / len, ny = py / len;
  // Square corners
  const SQ = [A, C, {x: C.x + dx/len * 0 + nx * len, y: C.y + ny * len}, {x: A.x + nx * len, y: A.y + ny * len}];
  // Adjust: third corner is C + perpendicular*len; fourth is A + perpendicular*len
  SQ[2] = {x: C.x + nx * len, y: C.y + ny * len};
  SQ[3] = {x: A.x + nx * len, y: A.y + ny * len};
  const pathStr = `M ${SQ[0].x},${SQ[0].y} L ${SQ[1].x},${SQ[1].y} L ${SQ[2].x},${SQ[2].y} L ${SQ[3].x},${SQ[3].y} Z`;
  svg.append("path").attr("d", pathStr)
    .attr("fill", "#f8bbd0").attr("opacity", 0.6).attr("stroke", "#c2185b");
  // Centroid for label
  const cx = (SQ[0].x + SQ[1].x + SQ[2].x + SQ[3].x) / 4;
  const cy = (SQ[0].y + SQ[1].y + SQ[2].y + SQ[3].y) / 4;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", cx).attr("y", cy + 6)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#c2185b")
    .text(`c² = ${(c**2).toFixed(1)}`);

  // Triangle outline
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", `${A.x},${A.y} ${B.x},${B.y} ${C.x},${C.y}`)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // Right-angle marker
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", B.x).attr("y", B.y - 12).attr("width", 12).attr("height", 12)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#1f2933");

  // Side labels
  svg.append("text").attr("x", B.x - 12).attr("y", (A.y + B.y)/2)
    .attr("text-anchor", "end").attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`a = ${aSide}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", (B.x + C.x)/2).attr("y", B.y + 18)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`b = ${bSide}`);

  // Equation
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W - 30).attr("y", 30)
    .attr("text-anchor", "end").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`a² + b² = c²`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W - 30).attr("y", 52)
    .attr("text-anchor", "end").attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-family", "monospace").attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`${aSide**2} + ${bSide**2} = ${(c**2).toFixed(1)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W - 30).attr("y", 70)
    .attr("text-anchor", "end").attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-family", "monospace").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`c = √${(c**2).toFixed(1)} = ${c.toFixed(2)}`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

The blue square + the green square = the pink square. Always. That's
the **Pythagorean theorem**.

## Wonder

1. Set $a = 3, b = 4$. The hypotenuse comes out to *exactly* 5. Check:
   $9 + 16 = 25 = 5^2$.
2. What if $a = 5, b = 5$? What is $c$?
3. The theorem only works for **right** triangles. Why might the right
   angle be special?

## Discover

### Problem 1

For a right triangle with legs $a = 5$ and $b = 12$, find $c$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c^2 = 5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169$. $c = \sqrt{169} = 13$.

A 5-12-13 triangle is another **Pythagorean triple** — three integers
satisfying $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. There are infinitely many.
</details>

### Problem 2

For a right triangle with hypotenuse $c = 10$ and one leg $a = 6$, find
the other leg.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Rearrange: $b^2 = c^2 - a^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$b^2 = 100 - 36 = 64$. $b = 8$. (Yet another Pythagorean triple:
6-8-10, which is a 3-4-5 scaled by 2.)
</details>

### Problem 3

What is the diagonal of a square with side 1?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The diagonal is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with both legs of
length 1.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$. $d = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

This is the irrational number from [Lesson 9](../09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd) — and
its first geometric appearance in human history was as the diagonal of
a unit square. The Pythagoreans (who proved $\sqrt{2}$ irrational)
encountered it precisely *here*.
</details>

### Problem 4

Use the coordinate plane (Lesson 4): find the **distance** from
$(1, 2)$ to $(4, 6)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sketch the segment. The horizontal change is $4 - 1 = 3$, the vertical
change is $6 - 2 = 4$. The distance is the hypotenuse of a right
triangle with legs 3 and 4.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d^2 = 3^2 + 4^2 = 25$, so $d = 5$.

In general, the distance between $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is
$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}.$$

This is the **distance formula** — Pythagoras applied to coordinates.
Together with [Lesson 4](../04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd) (the
plane), this completes the bridge between geometry and algebra.
</details>

### Problem 5

A 5-metre ladder leans against a wall, with its base **3 m from the
wall**. How high up the wall does it reach?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The ladder, the wall, and the ground form a right triangle with legs
$h$ (height) and 3 (distance from wall), and hypotenuse 5 (ladder
length).
$h^2 + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow h^2 = 16 \Rightarrow h = 4$ m.

The classic ladder problem. Pythagoras shows up in carpentry,
construction, surveying, navigation — anywhere you need a quick
right-triangle calculation.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Pythagorean Theorem.** In a right triangle with legs $a, b$ and
hypotenuse $c$ (the side opposite the right angle, always the longest):
$$a^2 + b^2 = c^2.$$

**A Pythagorean triple** is a set of three positive integers
$(a, b, c)$ satisfying this equation. Examples: $(3, 4, 5), (5, 12, 13),
(8, 15, 17), (7, 24, 25)$ — and any positive multiple of these.

**Distance formula** in the coordinate plane: distance from $(x_1, y_1)$
to $(x_2, y_2)$ is
$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}.$$

The theorem also has a **converse**: if $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ for the three
sides of a triangle, then the triangle is right-angled.

:::

> *Square the legs, add — that equals the square of the hypotenuse;*
> *Take the square root for the actual length;*
> *Right angles only — but the world is full of them.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A field is rectangular, **40 m by 30 m**. A walkway runs diagonally
> from one corner to the opposite. How long is it?

The diagonal of a rectangle is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with
legs equal to the rectangle's sides:
$$d^2 = 40^2 + 30^2 = 1600 + 900 = 2500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = 50 \text{ m}.$$

**Answer: 50 m.** (Notice this is a 30-40-50 right triangle — a
3-4-5 scaled by 10.) The diagonal saves 20 m versus walking around
two sides (30 + 40 = 70).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $a = 6, b = 8$. Find $c$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c^2 = 36 + 64 = 100$, $c = 10$.
</details>

**W2.** $c = 17, a = 8$. Find $b$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$b^2 = 289 - 64 = 225$, $b = 15$.
</details>

**W3.** Distance from $(0, 0)$ to $(3, 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A right triangle has legs 9 and 12. Find the hypotenuse.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c^2 = 81 + 144 = 225$, $c = 15$. (Another 3-4-5 multiple, scaled by 3.)
</details>

**P2.** Distance from $(-2, 1)$ to $(4, 9)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\Delta x = 6$, $\Delta y = 8$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10$.
</details>

**P3.** A TV is advertised as **42 inches** — that's the *diagonal*
length of the screen. If the screen has aspect ratio 16:9 (width:height),
what are the actual width and height?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let width = $16k$, height = $9k$. Diagonal: $\sqrt{(16k)^2 + (9k)^2}
= 42$. Solve for $k$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(16k)^2 + (9k)^2 = 42^2 \Rightarrow 256k^2 + 81k^2 = 1764 \Rightarrow
337 k^2 = 1764 \Rightarrow k^2 \approx 5.235 \Rightarrow k \approx 2.288$.

Width $\approx 16 \times 2.288 \approx 36.6$ inches. Height $\approx
9 \times 2.288 \approx 20.6$ inches. Quick check: $\sqrt{36.6^2 + 20.6^2}
\approx \sqrt{1340 + 424} \approx 42$ ✓.

TV manufacturers quote the diagonal because it's slightly larger and
sounds more impressive, and because it's a single number.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **3D distance.** A cuboid measures 3 × 4 × 12. What is the length
of its space diagonal (corner to opposite corner through the interior)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First find the diagonal of the base ($3 \times 4$ rectangle). Then the
space diagonal is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs (base
diagonal) and 12.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Base diagonal: $\sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$.
Space diagonal: $\sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$.

**Generalised:** for a cuboid $a \times b \times c$, space diagonal $=
\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$ — the 3D Pythagorean theorem. By induction,
in any dimension $n$ the diagonal of an $n$-orthotope is the square
root of the sum of squared edges.

This is exactly the formula every game engine uses to compute distances
in 3D space, millions of times per frame.
</details>

**S2.** **Why does the theorem work?** Below is one of dozens of known
proofs. Consider a large square of side $a + b$. Inside, four right
triangles (legs $a, b$, hypotenuse $c$) are arranged to leave an
inner square of side $c$. The total area is $(a + b)^2$. The four
triangles contribute $4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} ab = 2ab$. So the inner
square has area $(a + b)^2 - 2ab = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 - 2ab = a^2 + b^2$.
But it is also $c^2$. Hence $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.

What is the *one move* that makes this proof work?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Counting the same area two different ways.** Once as $(a + b)^2$
minus four triangles; once as $c^2$. Both expressions describe the same
inner region, so they must be equal.

Counting area two ways is one of the most powerful proof techniques in
geometry. Pythagoras' theorem has hundreds of proofs in this style.
The Indian mathematician Bhāskara II (12th century) gave a famously
elegant one-word proof: he drew the rearrangement above and wrote
just "**Behold!**" — a complete proof.

Tier 3 will revisit Pythagoras using *similar triangles* (Lesson 13)
for a proof from a totally different direction.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$a + b = c$."** No — that's the *sum of legs*, not the
   hypotenuse. $3 + 4 = 7$, but the hypotenuse is 5. The squares are
   essential.
2. **"Pythagoras applies to all triangles."** **No** — only **right**
   triangles. For general triangles you need the *Law of Cosines* (Tier
   3 trig), which generalises Pythagoras with a correction term.
3. **"The hypotenuse is *always* labelled $c$."** It's the *longest*
   side, opposite the right angle. The labelling $a, b$ for legs and
   $c$ for hypotenuse is convention.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you sketch a right triangle and explain why $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$,
   using the area-counting argument?
2. Without scrolling back, can you find the distance from $(2, 1)$ to
   $(7, 13)$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $\sqrt{25 + 144} = 13$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[11 · Polynomials](../11-polynomials/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (3D distance) — the formula generalises
  cleanly.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P4* and the worked example.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)**
- **[09 · Square Roots and Irrationals](../09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — Trigonometry** — sine, cosine, tangent are ratios of
  right-triangle sides; Pythagoras gives the identity
  $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$.
- **Tier 4 — 3D Geometry & Vectors** — the distance formula extends
  to 3D and beyond; magnitude of a vector is a Pythagorean
  computation.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** Distance, collision detection,
camera-to-object measurement — every frame of every 3D game runs
millions of Pythagorean calculations. Many engines compute the
*squared* distance to skip the square root for speed.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** **Euclidean distance** between feature
vectors is the basic similarity measure for many ML algorithms
(k-nearest-neighbours, k-means clustering). It is Pythagoras
generalised to many dimensions.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Pythagoras' theorem generalises in
beautiful directions: to inner-product spaces (Tier 5+ linear
algebra), to non-Euclidean geometries (different formulas hold on
spheres and hyperbolic spaces), and to abstract metric spaces.
It is the *defining* formula for Euclidean geometry.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Construction (squaring corners with a 3-4-5
triangle is an ancient builder's trick), navigation (great-circle
distances), surveying, archaeology — Pythagoras has been
indispensable in human practice for over 2500 years.
:::
